subject: [PATCH v3] memcg: cache obj_stock by memcg, not by objcg pointer

reviews.author: Qi Zheng
reviews.summary: Reviewer Qi Zheng acknowledged the patch as a temporary fix, expressed no further concerns or suggestions, and gave an Acked-by tag.
reviews.raw_body:
I think this is fine as a temporary fix:

Acked-by: Qi Zheng <qi.zheng@linux.dev>

Thanks!

reviews.author: Harry Yoo
reviews.summary: Reviewer Harry Yoo questioned the temporary fix, pointing out that once per-node kmem accounting is introduced, the assumption that 'stock->nr_bytes are fungible across per-node objcgs of one memcg' no longer holds. He suggested reverting this patch and addressing the issue with a multi-objcg percpu stock cache instead.
reviews.raw_body:
... because once per-node kmem accounting is introduced,
"stock->nr_bytes are fungible across per-node objcgs of one memcg"
no longer holds?

And the follow-up plain is to revert this and address it with a 
multi-objcg percpu stock [1], similar to a multi-memcg percpu charge 
cache we have now, right? (regardless of per-node kmem accounting's 
progress)

If this temporary fix imposes other potential correctness issues, would 
it make sense to land [1] in mainline before the next LTS release and 
skip this temporary fix?

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/oe-lkp/agtPMpQK2jXdQAY4@linux.dev

-- 
Cheers,
Harry / Hyeonggon

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledges a significant performance regression and suggests that the patch cannot be ignored, implying that further work is needed to address it.
reviews.raw_body:
The full clean solution might take one more cycle and I think we can not just
ignore 67% regression on 7.1.

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged a potential issue with reverting the patch, citing re-introduction of node LRU locking in the objcg reparenting path.

Author acknowledges that a fix is needed for the regression in future kernels and suggests three options: doing nothing, applying the current patch, or backporting the multi-objcg path to kernel 7.1.
reviews.raw_body:
The issue with that revert is that we reintroduce all node lru locking in the
objcg reparenting path.

---

I am waiting for Oliver to test the multi-objcg patch I sent. If that also
resolves the regression then we have one more option i.e. backport that to 7.1
to fix the regression.

So to summarize, for future kernels we will be having multi-objcg in some form.
For 7.1, we have to decide between (1) do nothing (2) this patch or (3) backport
the multi-objcg path to 7.1.

Andrew, please don't send this patch to Linus until we decide on the option.

2026-05-19.patch_summary: This patch fixes a performance regression in the Linux kernel's memory control group (memcg) implementation, specifically when dealing with cross-NUMA workloads. The issue arises from the fact that obj_stock_pcp caches are keyed by pointer to obj_cgroup instead of memcg, leading to unnecessary drains and misses on every refill and consume operation. To fix this, the patch changes the cache keying to use memcg instead of obj_cgroup pointer, allowing siblings to share the reserve without re-caching. This temporary fix will be reverted when per-node kmem accounting is introduced.

reviews.author: Harry Yoo
reviews.summary: Reviewer Harry Yoo expressed concern about the per-node obj_cgroup change introduced in v5, citing an unexpected last-minute regression and correctness concerns, and suggested reverting this change for v7.1 until a cleaner solution is available.
reviews.raw_body:
That is valid point, unfortunately.

One more thing I have to ask... for v7.1, wouldn't it be a safer option 
to revert the per-node object change and re-introduce it once we have a 
cleaner solution?

This change was introduced in v5, but the implementation before v4 had 
been exposed in -next for a while, and I think we don't have enough 
justification to keep the per-node objcgs change, at least for v7.1, 
given that we have an unexpected last-minute regression and
correctness concerns (albeit slight).

-- 
Cheers,
Harry / Hyeonggon

reviews.author: Harry Yoo
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Harry Yoo questioned the severity of all-node locking issues in the patch, suggesting that it might not be a significant problem even for large servers due to the independent critical section for reparenting.

Reviewer Harry Yoo suggested that if per-node objcg becomes a requirement in the future, caching by memcg instead of objcg pointer might not be the best solution.

Reviewer Harry Yoo approved the patch, responding with a simple 'Ack.' and 'Thanks!'
sentiment_signals: requested consideration for future kernel requirements
reviews.raw_body:
I'm not sure the problems with all-node locking are serious enough to 
rule it out as an option for 7.1.

It is not ideal, but given that the critical section for reparenting is 
independent of folio count, would this actually be a significant problem 
in practice? (even large servers rarely go beyond 8 NUMA nodes...)

---

Yeah, If per-node objcg is required in future kernels anyway, this 
option would be more ideal (if available).

---

Ack.

Thanks!

-- 
Cheers,
Harry / Hyeonggon

2026-05-20.patch_summary: This patch fixes a performance regression in the Linux kernel's memory control group (memcg) implementation, specifically when dealing with cross-NUMA workloads. The issue arises from the fact that obj_stock_pcp caches are keyed by pointer to obj_cgroup instead of memcg, leading to unnecessary drains and misses on every refill and consume operation. To fix this, the patch changes the cache keying to use memcg instead of obj_cgroup pointer, allowing siblings to share the reserve without re-caching. This temporary fix will be reverted when per-node kmem accounting is introduced.

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary: Author suggests dropping the patch from mm-tree, citing that the multi-objcg series will address the issue in a future release and can be backported to non-LTS versions.
reviews.raw_body:
Here is my suggestion on how to proceed.

1. Let's drop this patch (memcg: cache obj_stock by memcg, not by objcg pointer)
   from mm-tree.

2. Let's not add anything to 7.1.

3. 7.2+ will have the multi-objcg series [1] and the patches will have fixes
   tag. If someone uses non-LTS 7.1, they can easily find those patches and can
   backport them.

Please let me know if there are any concerns.


[1] https://lore.kernel.org/20260522011908.1669332-1-shakeel.butt@linux.dev/
